From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

American actor (1940–2022)

| Emilio Delgado | |
| --- | --- |
| Delgado as Luis on *[Sesame Street](/wiki/Sesame_Street "Sesame Street")* | |
| Born | Emilio Ernest Delgado  (1940-05-08)May 8, 1940  [Calexico, California](/wiki/Calexico,_California "Calexico, California"), U.S. |
| Died | March 10, 2022(2022-03-10) (aged 81)  [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), U.S. |
| Occupation | Actor |
| Years active | 1968–2022 |
| Television | *[Sesame Street](/wiki/Sesame_Street "Sesame Street")* |
| Spouses | Barbara Snavely  ​  ​ (m. 1963; div. 1975)​      [Linda Moon](/wiki/Linda_Redfearn "Linda Redfearn")  ​  ​ (m. 1977; div. 1984)​      Carole Delgado  ​ (m. 1990)​ |
| Children | 2 |

**Emilio Ernest Delgado**[[1]](#cite_note-1) (May 8, 1940 – March 10, 2022) was an American actor best known for his role as [Luis](/wiki/The_Rodriguez_family_(Sesame_Street) "The Rodriguez family (Sesame Street)"), the Fix-it Shop owner, on the children's television series *[Sesame Street](/wiki/Sesame_Street "Sesame Street")*. He joined the cast of *Sesame Street* in 1971 and remained until his contract was not renewed, in late 2016, as part of [Sesame Workshop](/wiki/Sesame_Workshop "Sesame Workshop")'s retooling of the series.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Following his departure, the workshop stated that Delgado would continue to represent them at public events.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Delgado also appeared as Luis in the TV special *[Sesame Street's 50th Anniversary Celebration](/wiki/Sesame_Street%27s_50th_Anniversary_Celebration "Sesame Street's 50th Anniversary Celebration")*.[[4]](#cite_note-4) He began his professional career in Los Angeles in 1968. Delgado lived in New York City with his wife Carole Delgado.

## Life and career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Emilio_Delgado&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Life and career")]

### Childhood, education and early roles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Emilio_Delgado&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Childhood, education and early roles")]

Delgado was born in [Calexico, California](/wiki/Calexico,_California "Calexico, California"), to Emilio Delgado and Carmen Rodriguez Delgado on May 8, 1940.[[5]](#cite_note-Patel-5)

He was raised in his grandparents' house in [Mexicali](/wiki/Mexicali "Mexicali"), Mexico, with his extended family, during part of his childhood.[[6]](#cite_note-calarts-6) As a citizen of the United States, he would walk daily to attend a public school in Calexico.[[6]](#cite_note-calarts-6) He began working odd jobs as a ten-year-old and at his uncle's bicycle shop at age 12. His family moved to [Glendale, California](/wiki/Glendale,_California "Glendale, California"),[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) when he was a teenager. During high school, three years of which he attended [Glendale High School](/wiki/Glendale_High_School_(Glendale,_California) "Glendale High School (Glendale, California)"), he became president of the Thespian Club; he played the trombone in the jazz band and he was a drum major in the marching band.[[6]](#cite_note-calarts-6) He also played acoustic guitar from his early teens throughout his lifetime, playing in folk groups during the 1960s and his 44 years on Sesame Street.

Delgado was "adamantly and morally opposed" to the [Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War"), but enlisted in the [California Army National Guard](/wiki/California_National_Guard "California National Guard") for six years, serving domestically.[[6]](#cite_note-calarts-6) A supply corporal, Delgado was deployed to the [Watts riots](/wiki/Watts_riots "Watts riots") in Los Angeles, in 1965, where he was "astonished to see weekend warriors being issued live ammunition to use against other Americans."[[6]](#cite_note-calarts-6)

Delgado began acting professionally in 1968, after nine years of "trying to knock doors down in [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles") to get in."[[9]](#cite_note-StreetGang-9) That year, he received his first [Equity](/wiki/Actors%27_Equity_Association "Actors' Equity Association") job in a [summer stock play](/wiki/Summer_stock_theatre "Summer stock theatre") starring [Martha Raye](/wiki/Martha_Raye "Martha Raye"),[[6]](#cite_note-calarts-6) and later he was cast in the first Mexican-American [soap opera](/wiki/Soap_opera "Soap opera"), *Cancion de la Raza*.[[10]](#cite_note-SanAntonio-10)

Befriending actor Sergei Tschernisch at Los Angeles theatre company Inner City Repertory, Delgado learned of the new theatre program at [CalArts](/wiki/CalArts "CalArts"), led by Provost [Herb Blau](/wiki/Herbert_Blau "Herbert Blau"). While already a professional actor as of his 1970 enrollment, Delgado praised Blau's methods, suggesting his [avant-garde](/wiki/Avant-garde "Avant-garde") method was "amazing."[[6]](#cite_note-calarts-6)

As of 1970, he was the artistic director of the new Mexican-American Centre of Creative Arts, which taught Chicano high school and college students from the basement of the Euclid Heights Community Centre in [East Los Angeles](/wiki/East_Los_Angeles,_California "East Los Angeles, California").[[11]](#cite_note-11) Delgado told the *[Los Angeles Times](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Times "Los Angeles Times")*: "We are 100% positive in our approach, and we are uncompromising in our belief that our kids will come away thinking of themselves as artists. Nothing is going to stop us from attaining our identification in American society."[[12]](#cite_note-12)

Delgado had a guest role in an October 1970 episode *[Storefront Lawyers](/wiki/Storefront_Lawyers "Storefront Lawyers")* before being cast as a series regular in *Angie's Garage* in November 1970. The new children's series focused on serving Mexican-American children. He was billed as a singer-guitarist.[[13]](#cite_note-13) He speculated that *Sesame Street* producers discovered him through the series.[[9]](#cite_note-StreetGang-9)

Delgado recorded voice-over work in both English and Spanish.[[14]](#cite_note-HC-journey-2020-14)

### Early years on *Sesame Street* (1971–1988)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Emilio_Delgado&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early years on Sesame Street (1971–1988)")]

On *Sesame Street*, his character Luis, along with [Raul Julia](/wiki/Raul_Julia "Raul Julia") as Rafael, were the first human additions to the original cast. Luis was a [handyman](/wiki/Handyman "Handyman"), and an aspiring writer, who debuted on the show in 1971 simultaneously with Julia. Together they ran the L&R Fix-It Shop until Julia left the show after one season, and Luis ran the Fix-It Shop alone from then on.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The enormous popularity of *[Sesame Street](/wiki/Sesame_Street "Sesame Street")* created a barrage of groups providing input on the curriculum in its second season.[[15]](#cite_note-15) During the season, the program attempted to teach Spanish to children whose mother tongue was English. Producer [Jon Stone](/wiki/Jon_Stone "Jon Stone") told *[The Pittsburgh Press](/wiki/The_Pittsburgh_Press "The Pittsburgh Press")* that their attempts were "a disaster. It was tokenism at best, and condescension at worst."[[16]](#cite_note-Post-pg-5-16) For the third season, the show rebooted their efforts, adding Puerto Ricans and Chicanos, and creating new Spanish segments.[[16]](#cite_note-Post-pg-5-16) Seven new cast members were added at the start of the season, including Delgado, Panchito Gómez, [Raul Julia](/wiki/Raul_Julia "Raul Julia"), and [Sonia Manzano](/wiki/Sonia_Manzano "Sonia Manzano").[[17]](#cite_note-17) Delgado was still enrolled at CalArts when he was cast.[[6]](#cite_note-calarts-6) The character has been described as the "antithesis" of the Mexican and Latino stereotypes that proliferated television at the time, as he was "an honest, upstanding, hard-working, affable person."[[6]](#cite_note-calarts-6) Delgado expected the role to last one or two seasons.[[6]](#cite_note-calarts-6)

Delgado joined the series' live events by at least 1972, when he performed with the [Jackson Symphony Orchestra](/wiki/Jackson_Symphony_Orchestra_(Michigan) "Jackson Symphony Orchestra (Michigan)").[[18]](#cite_note-18)

Delgado was named the coordinator of the [Children's Television Workshop](/wiki/Children%27s_Television_Workshop "Children's Television Workshop")'s Bilingual Task Force, and he was sent across the country to meet with groups. The efforts were to lead to further updates to content in season 4.[[10]](#cite_note-SanAntonio-10) He commented in 1972 that the series didn't "teach Spanish, we teach in Spanish."[[19]](#cite_note-19)

Delgado is believed to have played "the same role on American television longer than any other Mexican-American actor," according to CalArts.[[6]](#cite_note-calarts-6)

### Later seasons on *Sesame Street* (1988–2016)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Emilio_Delgado&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Later seasons on Sesame Street (1988–2016)")]

During *Sesame Street*'s 19th season, first aired in 1988, Luis fell in love with [Maria](/wiki/Maria_Figueroa_Rodriguez "Maria Figueroa Rodriguez"), played by [Sonia Manzano](/wiki/Sonia_Manzano "Sonia Manzano"), and married her. The characters Luis and Maria taught viewers about [Hispanic](/wiki/Hispanic "Hispanic") culture and language throughout their shared run of the show. According to Delgado, "to this day, there are fans out there who want to believe that the Luis and Maria wedding episode in 1988 was real, but the fact of the matter is, it was just terrific acting."[[6]](#cite_note-calarts-6)

Delgado later appeared at various pop culture conventions, often under the brand "Humans of Sesame Street".[[20]](#cite_note-20) Delgado also reprised the role of Luis in the TV special *[Sesame Street's 50th Anniversary Celebration](/wiki/Sesame_Street%27s_50th_Anniversary_Celebration "Sesame Street's 50th Anniversary Celebration")*.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

Delgado also performed in live shows throughout his *Sesame Street* career, singing the songs of *Sesame Street* and entertaining thousands of children and their families.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

### Additional roles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Emilio_Delgado&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Additional roles")]

Delgado took guest roles on other series while *Sesame Street* was not taping. Notably, he had a recurring role as national news editor Rubin Castillo on the television series *[Lou Grant](/wiki/Lou_Grant_(TV_series) "Lou Grant (TV series)")*.[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[6]](#cite_note-calarts-6)

Delgado starred in *Quixote Nuevo* that premiered in 2018 at [CalShakes](/wiki/CalShakes "CalShakes") Theater in Berkeley, California, then went on tour in 2019 to [Hartford Stage](/wiki/Hartford_Stage "Hartford Stage") in Connecticut, Boston's [Huntington Theatre](/wiki/Huntington_Theatre "Huntington Theatre") and Houston's [Alley Theatre](/wiki/Alley_Theatre "Alley Theatre") production (2020) of [Octavio Solis](/wiki/Octavio_Solis "Octavio Solis"), a modern Chicano adaptation of *[Don Quixote](/wiki/Don_Quixote "Don Quixote")*.[[14]](#cite_note-HC-journey-2020-14)

In Los Angeles, he was a company member of Inner City Rep, The Group Repertory, and LA Repertory. Some of his New York City theatre credits include *Floating Home* (HExTC), *Boxing 2000* (Richard Maxwell NYC Players), *Dismiss All the Poets* (New York Fringe Festival 2002), [Nilo Cruz](/wiki/Nilo_Cruz "Nilo Cruz")'s adaptation of *[A Very Old Man with Enormous Wings](/wiki/A_Very_Old_Man_with_Enormous_Wings "A Very Old Man with Enormous Wings")* [Shakespeare Theatre of New Jersey](/wiki/Shakespeare_Theatre_of_New_Jersey "Shakespeare Theatre of New Jersey"), Dinosaurios (IATI), *Night Over Taos* [INTAR Theatre](/wiki/INTAR_Theatre "INTAR Theatre") an adaptation *[How the García Girls Lost Their Accents](/wiki/How_the_Garc%C3%ADa_Girls_Lost_Their_Accents "How the García Girls Lost Their Accents")* [Round House Theatre](/wiki/Round_House_Theatre "Round House Theatre") and Emilio appeared in the role of King Claudius in [Asolo Repertory Theatre](/wiki/Asolo_Repertory_Theatre "Asolo Repertory Theatre") production of *Hamlet, Prince of Cuba*, with alternating performances in English and Spanish, where one reviewer wrote that Delgado "is equally brilliant as King Claudius".[[24]](#cite_note-24)

His other television appearances include *[House of Cards](/wiki/House_of_Cards_(American_TV_series) "House of Cards (American TV series)")*, *[Law & Order](/wiki/Law_%26_Order "Law & Order")*, *[Law & Order: Criminal Intent](/wiki/Law_%26_Order:_Criminal_Intent "Law & Order: Criminal Intent")*, and *[Law & Order: Special Victims Unit](/wiki/Law_%26_Order:_Special_Victims_Unit "Law & Order: Special Victims Unit")*. He was a regular cast member of *[Lou Grant](/wiki/Lou_Grant_(television_series) "Lou Grant (television series)")* and the short-lived *[Born to the Wind](/wiki/Born_to_the_Wind "Born to the Wind")*. He also appeared in episodes of *[Police Story](/wiki/Police_Story_(1973_TV_series) "Police Story (1973 TV series)")*; *[Hawaii Five-O](/wiki/Hawaii_Five-O_(1968_TV_series) "Hawaii Five-O (1968 TV series)")*; and *[Quincy, M.E.](/wiki/Quincy,_M.E. "Quincy, M.E.")*

Delgado's other creative endeavor was to sing and record with the band [Pink Martini](/wiki/Pink_Martini "Pink Martini"). He performed with the band at [Carnegie Hall](/wiki/Carnegie_Hall "Carnegie Hall") and [Town Hall](/wiki/Town_Hall_(New_York_City) "Town Hall (New York City)") in New York City, The [Hollywood Bowl](/wiki/Hollywood_Bowl "Hollywood Bowl") in Los Angeles,[[25]](#cite_note-25) in [Woodinville, Washington](/wiki/Woodinville,_Washington "Woodinville, Washington") at the Chateau Ste. Michelle Winery, and in [Portland, Oregon](/wiki/Portland,_Oregon "Portland, Oregon") at the [Arlene Schnitzer Concert Hall](/wiki/Arlene_Schnitzer_Concert_Hall "Arlene Schnitzer Concert Hall"), [Crystal Ballroom](/wiki/Crystal_Ballroom_(Portland,_Oregon) "Crystal Ballroom (Portland, Oregon)") and the [Oregon Zoo](/wiki/Oregon_Zoo "Oregon Zoo"). He appears on their album *[Splendor in the Grass](/wiki/Splendor_in_the_Grass_(album) "Splendor in the Grass (album)")*, in which he recorded the song "[Sing](/wiki/Sing_(Joe_Raposo_song) "Sing (Joe Raposo song)")", a duet with [China Forbes](/wiki/China_Forbes "China Forbes").[[26]](#cite_note-26)

In the latter years of his life, just before his death, he also served on the board of directors at the [Bayard Rustin Center for Social Justice](/wiki/Bayard_Rustin_Center_for_Social_Justice "Bayard Rustin Center for Social Justice"), an LGBTQIA+ safe-space, community activist center, and educational bridge dedicated to honoring [Bayard Rustin](/wiki/Bayard_Rustin "Bayard Rustin") through their mission and good works.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

### Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Emilio_Delgado&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Personal life")]

Emilio and Carole were married in Bermuda, January 28, 1990. Delgado has a daughter, Lauren, and a son, Aram Delgado, from his previous marriage, who died on December 28, 2023.[[6]](#cite_note-calarts-6) He has one grandson, Eladio Delgado.

Delgado's voice has been described as "deep, smooth and sonorous, his words precise and deliberate."[[14]](#cite_note-HC-journey-2020-14)

### Recognitions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Emilio_Delgado&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Recognitions")]

Delgado received The Impact Award from the [National Hispanic Media Coalition](/wiki/National_Hispanic_Media_Coalition "National Hispanic Media Coalition"), The [HOLA](/wiki/Hispanic_Organization_of_Latin_Actors "Hispanic Organization of Latin Actors") Ilka Award, and the Keys to the cities of Evansville and Omaha.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

### Death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Emilio_Delgado&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Death")]

In December 2020, Delgado was diagnosed with [multiple myeloma](/wiki/Multiple_myeloma "Multiple myeloma").[[5]](#cite_note-Patel-5) He died from the disease at his home in [Manhattan, New York City](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan"), on March 10, 2022, at the age of 81.[[5]](#cite_note-Patel-5)[[29]](#cite_note-29)

## Filmography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Emilio_Delgado&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Filmography")]

* *[I Will Fight No More Forever](/wiki/I_Will_Fight_No_More_Forever "I Will Fight No More Forever")* - TV movie - [Ollokot](/wiki/Ollokot "Ollokot") (1975)
* *[Born to the Wind](/wiki/Born_to_the_Wind "Born to the Wind")* - [White Bull](/wiki/White_Bull "White Bull") (1982)
* *[Sesame Street Presents: Follow That Bird](/wiki/Sesame_Street_Presents:_Follow_That_Bird "Sesame Street Presents: Follow That Bird")* - [Luis Rodriguez](/wiki/The_Rodriguez_family_(Sesame_Street) "The Rodriguez family (Sesame Street)") (1985)
* *Reggie's Prayer* - Stadium Announcer (1996)
* *[The Adventures of Elmo in Grouchland](/wiki/The_Adventures_of_Elmo_in_Grouchland "The Adventures of Elmo in Grouchland")* - Luis Rodriguez (1999)
* *Perhaps Tomorrow* - Zelgai (2010)
* *[A Case of You](/wiki/A_Case_of_You_(film) "A Case of You (film)")* - Roberto (2013)
* *[Peeples](/wiki/Peeples_(film) "Peeples (film)")* - Chief Oneka (2013)
* *Montauk* - Eduardo (2013)
* *Luz Marina* - Carlos (2018)
* *iGilbert* - Rodolfo Gonzalez (2021)
* *[Street Gang: How We Got to Sesame Street](/wiki/Street_Gang:_How_We_Got_to_Sesame_Street "Street Gang: How We Got to Sesame Street")* - Self (2021)

## Television

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Emilio_Delgado&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Television")]

* *[Storefront Lawyers](/wiki/Storefront_Lawyers "Storefront Lawyers")* - episode - Survivors Will Be Prosecuted - Cadena (1970)
* *[Sesame Street](/wiki/Sesame_Street "Sesame Street")* - [Luis Rodriguez](/wiki/The_Rodriguez_family_(Sesame_Street) "The Rodriguez family (Sesame Street)") (1971–2016 & 2019)
* *[Cannon](/wiki/Cannon_(TV_series) "Cannon (TV series)")* - episode - Cry Wolf - Dr. Guiterrez (1976)
* *[Police Story](/wiki/Police_Story_(1973_TV_series) "Police Story (1973 TV series)")* - episode - Spanish Class - Agitator #1 (1976)
* *[Delvecchio](/wiki/Delvecchio_(TV_series) "Delvecchio (TV series)")* - episode - The High Price of Justice - Attorney (1977)
